topics: Holy Spirit brings life to the dead
t7EVD-dn_34.summary: Bishop Heward-Mills teaches that the Holy Spirit begins as a well inside believers for personal blessing but must break out to become a river flowing outward to bring life, healing, and salvation to others. He contrasts temple Christians (alive with the Spirit flowing out) with tomb Christians (outwardly religious but spiritually dead inside, living only on past experiences). The river of the Holy Spirit flows toward dead places like classrooms, workplaces, and hostels, bringing spiritual life to the lost, healing to the broken, and a great harvest of souls wherever believers allow it to flow through them.

FnAkGA39Vl4.summary: Bishop Joshua Heward-Mills teaches that backsliding means sliding backward in your relationship with God rather than moving forward, and that Christians are never standing still—they are either progressing or regressing spiritually. He explains that everything on earth is decaying and relationships with God require active maintenance, warning that many people start their Christian journey beautifully but slowly drift away without realizing it because there are no clear notifications or warnings when spiritual decline begins. Using the prophet Jeremiah's message and comparing spiritual relationships to romantic relationships, he emphasizes that believers must assess whether they are continuing with Christ or slowly falling away, noting that symptoms like prayerlessness, missing church, and losing passion indicate backsliding.

apy6uAEmco4.summary: BJosh teaches that the Holy Spirit is symbolized by oil in Scripture, which marks believers out as different from the world, brings divine light and guidance for life decisions, and transforms desires from worldly to godly. He emphasizes that many Christians remain unchanged because they lack a personal relationship with the Holy Spirit, not just knowledge about God, and that fresh oil from the Spirit is needed continuously to maintain spiritual light and direction. The anointing oil separates believers unto God as priests and kings, guides them through the darkness of life's decisions, and makes Christianity attractive by glorifying Jesus and changing what believers desire.

topics: Holy Spirit as person not force
HwB4-FYG0bk.summary: Bishop Joshua teaches that while all Christians have the Holy Spirit, most have not 'received' (Greek: lambano) Him as a personal companion they associate with and know intimately. He explains the Holy Spirit is not a force or power but a person with will, emotions, mind, and the ability to converse, using emblems throughout Scripture to reveal His personality. The sermon begins a series introducing believers to the person of the Holy Spirit so they can develop a friendship with Him rather than just knowing about Him.
HwB4-FYG0bk.notes: Sermon announces upcoming series on Holy Spirit emblems: oil (Mother's Day next week), dove, wind, mantle, and person (today's focus)

He emphasizes that Christians are like sheep among wolves facing continuous battles they cannot win in their own strength, but must invoke the name of Jehovah Sabaoth as David did against Goliath. ZO-OVUQejg0.summary: Bishop Joshua Heward-Mills teaches that backsliding has specific root causes including spiritual shallowness (lacking deep Bible-based convictions), emptiness (not being filled with the Holy Spirit), bitterness/unforgiveness, rebellion, and lust. He emphasizes that shallow Christians receive God's word with joy but have no roots, so they believe for a while but fall away in times of testing because their faith is based on following people rather than personal conviction in Scripture. He stresses that Christians must move from doing things out of fear to doing things out of love for God, developing deep spiritual roots through personal knowledge of God's Word rather than merely following pastors or other people.

topics: God works for His name's sake
TE-D2y1OIUY.summary: Bishop Heward-Mills teaches that Elohim means Creator God who makes something out of nothing, requiring no raw material, background, or perfect conditions to work in our lives. He emphasizes that God does everything for His name's sake (His own nature and character), not because we deserve it, and that knowing God's names helps us trust Him and run to Him for safety. He stresses the importance of remembering our Creator in youth because foundational decisions are made early in life, and we must allow the Holy Spirit time to hover and brood over us to transform our lives.

gnGQUpwt0Tk.summary: Bishop Heward-Mills teaches that only the Holy Spirit working within believers can transform them to become Christ-like and pleasing to God, not external religious behaviors or self-effort. He emphasizes that the Holy Spirit's work happens in two stages: first changing a person's desires to want what God wants, then giving them the power to do it, which occurs gradually through spiritual disciplines like prayer, Bible reading, and church attendance. He argues that true Christianity is not fighting between your will and God's will, but allowing the Holy Spirit to change you from within so that you genuinely desire and choose what pleases God.

topics: being planted in the house of God
hl4NXSiLFio.summary: Bishop Joshua teaches that loving God's house (the church) is God's primary love language, and those who claim to love God but don't faithfully attend church are not speaking God's love language and He does not enjoy them. He emphasizes that consistent church attendance every Sunday is essential to spiritual growth, comparing it to Solomon's experience with 1,000 women to illustrate how God can distinguish genuine love from empty words among billions of professing Christians. He teaches that being planted (not just visiting) in God's house causes believers to go from strength to strength and flourish, while those who only attend sporadically will eventually backslide and stop serving God altogether.

w6yFWOCbKIU.summary: Bishop Heward-Mills teaches that every Christian is not only saved but also called by God with a holy calling to do something specific for Him, and this calling comes with both purpose and grace. He emphasizes that 'many are called' means the majority—the church members, not just pastors—have a divine assignment they will answer for at the judgment seat of Christ. The message stresses that salvation and calling are inseparable (like a combo package), and Christians will be judged not for their sins (covered by Jesus' blood) but for what they did with their calling.

VuRH7nF1lkw.summary: Bishop Joshua teaches that every Christian is not only saved but also called by God with a holy calling, which means God is asking them to do something for Him according to His purpose and grace. He emphasizes that the majority of believers (not just pastors and leaders) are called and will stand before the judgment seat of Christ to give account for whether they fulfilled their calling. He warns that many Christians mistakenly think only pastors, apostles, prophets, evangelists, and teachers are called to do God's work, when in reality the 'many' refers to regular church members who have specific duties and assignments from God.

rQ694zXgHzo.summary: Bishop Joshua teaches that being filled with the Holy Spirit is like being drunk with alcohol—just as drinking one glass doesn't make you drunk, having the Holy Spirit doesn't mean you're filled unless He completely takes over your life. He emphasizes three prayer topics: praying for laborers to enter the harvest field, praying for the unsaved in our streets and workplaces, and praying against Satan's plans to harm church members. He uses the comparison of alcohol intoxication throughout to illustrate that Christians must move beyond merely possessing the Holy Spirit to being completely influenced and controlled by Him, living 'under the influence' of the Spirit rather than just having Him present.
